attributes.stem:
A 54-year-old man with severe dysphagia due to achalasia is barely able to manage to swallow both food and liquids and is rapidly losing weight. He is booked for a laparoscopic cardiomyotomy to definitively correct his achalasia. Two weeks prior to the planned procedure, he develops a deep vein thrombosis (DVT) in his right calf after prolonged bed rest recovering from an aspiration pneumonia. On ultrasound, the DVT does not extend above the knee. As a result, he is started on a six-week course of apixaban.

He is otherwise healthy and takes no other regular medications.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate plan of management during his operative admission?
options: Continue apixaban and proceed to surgery
Continue apixaban and delay surgery until he has finished the course of apixaban
Stop apixaban, insert inferior vena cava filter and proceed to surgery
Stop apixaban, start therapeutic subcutaneous low-molecular-weight heparin and proceed to surgery
Stop apixaban, give prothrombin complex concentrate and proceed to surgery
attributes.explanation:
Because the thrombus is acute (<4 weeks between onset and surgery), any interruption of anticoagulation would have an increased risk of clot extension, embolisation or new thrombus formation. Bridging should occur during this period for any interruption of therapy to manage this risk. Therefore, the only two reasonable options here are either to continue the apixaban and defer surgery until he has finished his course of the agent or to stop the apixaban and bridge with low-molecular-weight heparin. Because this patient cannot receive nutrition and is actively losing weight, the risk of delaying surgery likely outweighs the risk of peri-op thrombosis.

Stopping the apixaban and starting therapeutic subcutaneous low-molecular-weight heparin and the proceeding to surgery provides adequate prophylaxis against venous thromboembolism. Allowing the heparin to be held the night before surgery will reduce operative bleeding risk. The heparin can then be restarted postoperatively to provide prophylaxis for the remainder of the admission, and apixaban can be recommenced on discharge. This strategy is termed "bridging" anticoagulation, and heparin is often used to bridge patients on regular warfarin or DOACs (such as apixaban) due to its short half-life when compared to oral agents. In more minor surgery and that where the bleeding risk is minimal, bridging is not required and the DOAC can be held 12 hours prior to the surgical procedure.

It is important to note that this patient requires a higher ("therapeutic") dose of LMWH due to his recent VTE, whereas many patients without a history of recent VTE will receive a lower prophylactic dose.

It would be inappropriate to continue apixaban and proceed to surgery due to the significant risk of intra- or postoperative bleeding in anticoagulated patients.

In some patients, it may be appropriate to continue apixaban and delay surgery until he has finished the course of apixaban. However, this patient is rapidly deteriorating due to his achalasia and postponing his surgery for a further four weeks is inappropriate when options to safely proceed with surgery are available.

The option to stop apixaban, insert inferior vena cava filter and proceed to surgery could be considered in patients with a significant risk of pulmonary embolism who required a period without anticoagulation. However, this patient has suffered a provoked, distal DVT and the provoking factor has since resolved. This means that his current risk of pulmonary embolism is fairly low, and the risks of an IVC filter insertion are likely to outweigh the benefits.

The option to stop apixaban, give prothrombin complex concentrate and proceed to surgery would be appropriate in an unplanned emergency surgery. However, in an elective admission it is safer to bridge anticoagulation with heparin rather than actively reverse the apixaban with prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) due to the prothrombotic risk associated with PCCs.
learningPoints: Patients at risk of venous thromboembolism can safely be managed with heparin for VTE prophylaxis in the perioperative period.
references: Tran, H.A., Gibbs, H., Merriman, E., Curnow, J.L., Young, L., Bennett, A., Tan, C.W., Chunilal, S.D., Ward, C.M., Baker, R. and Nandurkar, H. (2019), New guidelines from the Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand for the diagnosis and management of venous thromboembolism. Med. J. Aust., 210: 227-235. https://doi.org/10.5694/mja2.50004
Sridhar R, Grigg AP. The perioperative management of anticoagulation. Aust Prescr 2000;23:13-6. https://doi.org/10.18773/austprescr.2000.011
Berry J, Patell R, Zwicker JI. The bridging conundrum: perioperative management of direct oral anticoagulants for venous thromboembolism. Thromb Haemost. 2023; 21:780–86.
